title: Papa Ramdas
meta_description: Swami Papa Ramdas (Vittal Rao, 1884–1963) was a Kerala-born sage who realised God through uninterrupted chanting of Ram's name, visited Ramana Maharshi at Arunachala, and founded Anandashram in Kanhangad.
body_html:
Early Life of Vittal Rao

Vittal Rao (later Swami Papa Ramdas) was born on Hanumat Jayanti, April 10th 1884 as the sixth son of the Brahmin couple Sri Balakrishna Rao and Lalitha Bai, at Hosdurg, Kerala. His parents led a simple life, full of devotion to God and dedication to austerities.

At twenty-five he married Rukma Bai who gave birth to a daughter Ramabai. Vittal Rao worked as a Spinning Master in several mills but was disturbed by seeing the exploitation of workmen. Because of his compassionate nature and lack of shrewdness, he failed at business and accumulated debts. His difficulties helped develop in him a longing for escape from worldly life. He reduced his sleep, spent the night in contemplation of Lord Ram, and wore khaddar; simplifying eating to bananas and boiled potatoes once daily.

Young Vittal Rao

Inspired by the teachings of Sri Ramakrishna, Swami Vivekananda and Swami Ram Tirtha, Papa became thoroughly convinced that God alone can give eternal peace. His father initiated him into the Ram mantram, and as the mantram took hold, Papa renounced worldly life and went forth as a mendicant sadhu.

Birth of Ramdas

On one morning in December 1922 Papa left home by train from Mangalore without knowing where he was going. He was directed to Srirangam where he donned the ochre robes of a sannyasin and assumed the new name Ramdas (servant of Ram). With the name of God constantly on his lips, Papa's travels took him to Tiruvannamalai.

Sri Ramana had recently moved out of the caves and taken up residency at his newly constructed ashram. Ramdas fell prostrate at his feet. Maharshi, turning his beautiful eyes toward Ramdas and looking intently for a few minutes, nodded his head to say he had blessed. "A thrill of inexpressible joy coursed through the frame of Ramdas, his whole body quivering like a leaf in the breeze."

Ramdas then spent nearly a month in a cave on the slopes of Arunachala in constant chanting of Ramnam. After twenty-one days, when he came out of the cave he saw a strange, all-pervasive light: everything was Ram and only Ram.

Swami the Tapasvi

Papa continued his travels all over India and finally settled down at Kasaragod, Kerala. It was here that Mother Krishnabai had his darshan and decided to dedicate her life to his service. They settled in Kanhangad where the present Anandashram was founded in the year 1931.

The Power of Nama Japa

As Swami Ramdas had attained realisation through uninterrupted chanting of the divine name of Ram, he always extolled the virtue of nama-japa: "The Divine Name is pregnant with a great power to transform the world. It can create light where there is darkness, love where there is hate... For, the Name is God himself."

Surrender to God

Papa emphasised the need of absolute surrender to the Divine Will: "His will is supreme. If we are conscious of this always, there is no struggle in life at all. When we surrender to God's will, we put all our burdens on Him. He is only too willing to carry everything."

Of Sri Ramana Maharshi he said: "Sri Ramana Maharshi was in all respects a remarkable saint. After realising the Eternal, he lived in the Eternal. His advent was a veritable blessing on this earth."

Development of Anandashram

Anandashram

Mother Krishnabai and Papa Ramdas

Between 1938 to 1949, Papa Ramdas remained at Anandashram, which quickly expanded to include new buildings, an elementary school (1942), a vocational training institute (1946), and a small hospital (1952).

Papa Ramdas Samadhi

On the evening of July 25th 1963, at Pope's 80th year, Ramdas had a severe heart attack. While lying down, he suddenly sat up chanting "Hari, Hari, Hari Ram." With the name of God on his lips Papa breathed his last.
notes: Subtitle is dates. Abridged from the writings of Swami Satchidananda.
